items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.correct: A recurring main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.).
items.misconception: A round where voices enter at different times.
items.why: Students confuse rondo (alternating refrain) with round (canon). Rondo: ABACA, ABACABA, etc. The A theme keeps coming back, like a refrain.
It's pp. “ff” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Gradually louder. “Suddenly loud” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many standard dynamic markings are between pp and ff inclusive?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
